At Google/OpenAI compensation levels, a "bit higher" salary buys almost nothing in well-being terms.

## Intrinsic interest and job characteristics

- The **Job Characteristics Model (Hackman & Oldham, 1976)** and decades of follow-up work show that skill variety, task significance, and autonomy are among the strongest predictors of intrinsic motivation and job satisfaction — substantially stronger than compensation.
- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)**: meta-analyses consistently show intrinsic motivation predicts well-being, persistence, and performance better than extrinsic motivators. Caveat relevant to you: SDT's three needs are autonomy, **competence**, and relatedness — leading a team can satisfy these too. "Interesting work" isn't the only intrinsic good.
- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012, 2017)**: interest–job congruence predicts performance and persistence, but the effect sizes are modest (ρ ≈ .20–.30) — meaningful, not overwhelming.
- **Wrzesniewski et al. (1997)**: people who view work as a "calling" (vs. a job or career-advancement vehicle) report significantly higher life and work satisfaction.
- On the flip side: chronic **boredom at work** is linked to turnover intentions, distress, and depressive symptoms (Harju, Hakanen & Schaufeli, 2014).

## Regret research — this cuts both ways for you

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1994, 1995)**: in the long run, people overwhelmingly regret **inactions** more than actions. Short-term regrets are about things done; long-term regrets are about chances not taken.
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)**: career is the **second most common regret domain** in life (after education), precisely because it's high-opportunity — many paths were available.
- This is important for your case: you said leading a team is "something I always wanted to do." The regret literature suggests that a long-held, unfulfilled aspiration is exactly the kind of thing people regret not pursuing decades later — potentially more than they'd regret a few years of less interesting work.

## Adaptation

- **Hedonic adaptation** research (Gilbert & Wilson; Lucas et al.) shows people adapt quickly to pay raises and titles, but much more slowly to the **daily experiential texture** of work. Kahneman's day-reconstruction work supports this: moment-to-moment experience of your tasks dominates remembered status markers. This favors interesting work — *unless* leading a team would itself be experientially rewarding day-to-day for you.

## Synthesis

The research, read honestly, says:

1. The salary difference is nearly irrelevant at your income level.
2. Interesting work is a genuinely strong, durable predictor of satisfaction — boredom is corrosive and you don't adapt to it.
3. **But** your situation isn't "interesting vs. money" — it's "interesting vs. a long-desired growth opportunity." The regret literature (inaction regret) and SDT (competence, growth) suggest unfulfilled aspirations are also a serious well-being cost.

One empirical question worth investigating yourself, since the literature can't answer it: is leadership at Google a realistic medium-term path? If yes, the trade-off may be false. If leading a team is only available via the less interesting role, you're genuinely in the territory where Gilovich's inaction-regret findings and Hackman-Oldham's task-interest findings point in opposite directions — and the deciding factor is whether "always wanted to" reflects a stable value or a status-tinged assumption you'd adapt away from.

answer:
There's actually a fair amount of relevant research. Here's what I can recall, organized by your sub-questions:

## Pay vs. job satisfaction

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff et al. (2010)** ran a meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction across ~90 samples. The correlation between salary level and job satisfaction was surprisingly weak — around **r = .15**. Pay *satisfaction* (feeling fairly paid) mattered somewhat more than absolute pay level.
- **Hedonic adaptation** research (e.g., work by Ed Diener, Andrew Clark's longitudinal studies using German panel data) shows people adapt to pay raises quite quickly — the satisfaction boost from a raise largely dissipates within 1–4 years. Note that a salary difference described as "a bit higher" would likely fall well within this adaptation effect.

## Interest fit and outcomes

- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012, 2017)** meta-analyses on *interest congruence* (how well your work matches your interests) found it predicts not just satisfaction but also **performance and persistence** — and the effect on performance was stronger than many traditional predictors. People doing work aligned with their interests perform better and stay longer.
- **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan) is the big framework here: intrinsic motivation (interest, enjoyment) and satisfaction of autonomy/competence/relatedness needs predict well-being and sustained engagement much more robustly than extrinsic rewards. There's also evidence that extrinsic rewards can *crowd out* intrinsic motivation (the "undermining effect," meta-analyzed by Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999 — though this is debated for high-stakes real-world settings).
- **Wrzesniewski et al. (1997)** on job/career/calling orientations: people who see their work as intrinsically meaningful ("calling") report higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward advancement or pay — even within the same occupation.
- **Amabile & Kramer's "Progress Principle"** (based on diary studies of knowledge workers): the single biggest driver of day-to-day positive inner work life was *making progress on meaningful work*. Day-to-day interest/meaning dominates over structural factors.

## Promotions and management roles

This is the complicating factor for your case:

- **Boyce & Oswald (2012)** found, using longitudinal data, that job promotions did **not** improve mental health on average — and there was some evidence of *worse* mental health afterward (stress, reduced leisure), at least in the short term.
- However, SDT-adjacent research on **goal attainment** matters here: achieving goals that are *self-concordant* (genuinely yours, not socially imposed) does increase well-being (Sheldon & Elliot, 1999). If leading a team is an authentic long-held aspiration rather than a status goal, the research would predict a real and more durable satisfaction gain. If it's more of an "I should want this" goal, the gain tends to be small or absent.

## Regret research

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret **inactions** (chances not taken) more than actions. Short-term regret skews toward actions; long-term regret skews toward "I never tried."
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)**: career is consistently among the **top life domains for regret** (along with education and romance) — and again, foregone opportunities dominate.
- Caveat: this cuts both ways for you — "I never tried leading a team" and "I gave up the work I loved" are both potential inaction/action regrets.

## Honest synthesis of what the literature implies

The research fairly consistently says: **interesting/meaningful work beats moderately higher pay** for long-run satisfaction (pay effects are small and adapt away; interest effects are larger and persistent). The genuinely ambiguous variable in your situation isn't the salary — it's the **team lead role**. The literature suggests that depends heavily on whether it's a self-concordant goal: if it is, attaining it predicts lasting well-being gains; if it's not, promotions per se don't make people happier and sometimes do the opposite.

One caveat: I'm recalling these from memory, so I'd encourage verifying the specific effect sizes before leaning on them heavily — but the directional findings (weak pay effects, strong interest-fit effects, mixed promotion effects, inaction regret) are well-replicated patterns.

## The honest synthesis

The research most clearly says: **the salary difference should get almost zero weight.** The real tradeoff is between two intrinsic goods — interesting work vs. a long-held aspiration to lead — and the literature doesn't adjudicate between those cleanly. The most decision-relevant findings are probably (a) self-concordance: figure out whether wanting to lead a team is authentically yours, and (b) the malleability of interest: "much less interesting" is a stronger constraint if the *domain* bores you than if the current *task description* does, since the latter often changes under your influence once you're in the role.

answer:
Great question, and there's actually a fair amount of relevant research. Here's what I can recall, organized by theme:

## Pay vs. intrinsic job characteristics

- **Judge et al. (2010)** conducted a meta-analysis of 92 studies and found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction was surprisingly weak (~0.15). People in higher-paying jobs were barely more satisfied with their jobs than people in lower-paying ones.
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around a certain income (then ~$75k in the US); beyond that, more money improves life *evaluation* but not daily experienced happiness. (Killingsworth's 2021 follow-up complicated this, but the effect of marginal income at high salaries remains small.) Given you're presumably well past any such threshold at either company, a "bit higher salary" likely has near-zero impact on well-being.
- **Hedonic adaptation**: salary increases are among the things people adapt to fastest (Clark et al., and work by Frederick & Loewenstein). The day-to-day texture of your work — what you actually think about for 8 hours — is something you re-experience constantly and adapt to less.

## Interest and meaningfulness of work

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)**: decades of evidence that autonomy, competence, and intrinsic motivation predict sustained engagement and well-being far better than extrinsic rewards. Extrinsic rewards can even crowd out intrinsic motivation (the "overjustification effect").
- **Nye et al. (2012, 2017)** meta-analyses on vocational interest fit: how well a job matches your interests predicts both performance and persistence — interest fit is a better predictor of performance than many traditional selection criteria.
- **Hu & Hirsh (2017)** and a well-known **BetterUp survey (Achor et al., 2018, HBR)** found people report being willing to give up a substantial portion of lifetime earnings (the BetterUp figure was ~23%) for work they find consistently meaningful.
- **Amabile & Kramer's "Progress Principle" (2011)**: analyzing ~12,000 diary entries from knowledge workers, the single biggest driver of positive inner work life was making progress on *meaningful work* — not recognition, incentives, or promotions.

## But — a complication relevant to your case

- **Wrzesniewski's work orientation research**: people who see work as a "calling" are most satisfied — but a calling isn't only about intellectual interest. Leading a team can be a calling too.
- Research on **promotion to management** is mixed: managers report higher meaning and autonomy on average, but also more stress and time pressure (Gallup data; studies by Johnston & Lee, 2013, found promotions improve job satisfaction only transiently and can worsen mental health). First-time managers in particular often experience a rough transition (Linda Hill's *Becoming a Manager*).
- **Boswell et al. (2005, 2009)** documented the "**honeymoon-hangover effect**": people who switch jobs experience a satisfaction spike that decays back toward baseline within about a year. So the excitement of the new role itself shouldn't be weighted heavily.

## Regret research

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* more than actions. Short-term regrets are about things done; long-term regrets are about chances not taken.
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)**: meta-analysis showing career and education are the top two life domains for regret — and the regrets are predominantly about foregone opportunities.
- This cuts both ways for you: not trying leadership (which you say you've always wanted) could become a classic inaction regret; but so could abandoning genuinely interesting research.

## Synthesis of what the literature suggests

The evidence is fairly consistent that **salary differences at your level will not meaningfully affect your happiness**, so you can almost discount that factor. The real trade-off is *interesting work* vs. *leadership opportunity* — and both are intrinsic motivators in SDT terms (competence/interest vs. autonomy/growth). The research can't resolve that for you, but it does suggest two things: (1) don't weight the salary or the novelty excitement, both of which adapt away; (2) the regret literature says the question to ask is which option you'd regret *not having tried* in 10 years — and notably, leadership opportunities tend to recur in a career, while truly interesting research problems at the frontier may be rarer.

One caveat: there's also good evidence on **affective forecasting errors** (Gilbert & Wilson) — we're systematically bad at predicting how choices will make us feel, and we overweight salient differences (like title and salary) over mundane daily experience. If you can, talk to people actually doing the OpenAI role day-to-day; "surrogation" (using others' actual experience) beats imagination as a forecasting method (Gilbert et al., 2009).

## Intrinsic interest is one of the strongest predictors

- The **Job Characteristics Model (Hackman & Oldham)** — one of the most replicated frameworks in organizational psychology — shows skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict satisfaction and internal motivation much more strongly than compensation.
- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)**: decades of evidence that intrinsic motivation (doing work because it's interesting) predicts well-being, persistence, and lower burnout, whereas pursuing extrinsic rewards (money, status) at the expense of intrinsic ones predicts *worse* well-being even when achieved (Kasser & Ryan; Niemiec et al., 2009 followed graduates and found those who attained extrinsic aspirations showed no well-being gain, sometimes more ill-being).

- **Wrzesniewski et al. (1997)** on job/career/calling orientations: people who see work as intrinsically meaningful report higher life satisfaction than those oriented toward advancement or pay — even within the same occupation.

This is the important nuance in your case:

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: long-term regrets are overwhelmingly about **inaction** — things not tried — rather than actions taken. **Roese & Summerville (2005)** found career is among the top life-regret domains, and regrets concentrate where people perceived opportunity but didn't act.

- **Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy (2005)** documented the **"honeymoon–hangover effect"**: job changers experience a satisfaction spike that typically erodes within a year, after which satisfaction is driven by the enduring characteristics of the work. The research would say: discount the salary difference heavily (weak, fast-adapting effect), weight the interestingness of daily work heavily (strong, durable effect), but don't dismiss the leadership aspiration — unacted-on aspirations are the classic source of long-term regret. answer:
Great question, and there's actually a decent body of research on this. Let me summarize what I can recall, with the caveat that you should verify specific citations before relying on them.

## Money vs. intrinsic factors

- **Judge et al. (2010)** published a meta-analysis ("The relationship between pay and job satisfaction") finding the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak — around r = .15. Pay raises produce satisfaction bumps that fade quickly (hedonic adaptation).
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found emotional well-being plateaus with income (the famous ~$75k figure, since revised upward and complicated by Killingsworth's 2021 follow-up). For someone already at a Google RE salary, a "bit higher" salary is very unlikely to move your day-to-day happiness.

## How much does "interesting work" matter?

- **The Job Characteristics Model (Hackman & Oldham)** and the large meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)** show that skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and challenge are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction — substantially stronger than compensation.
- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)**: well-being at work tracks satisfaction of autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Intrinsically motivating work predicts sustained engagement; extrinsic rewards don't compensate well for its absence.
- **Amabile & Kramer's "progress principle"** (diary studies of knowledge workers): the single biggest driver of positive inner work life was making progress on meaningful work.
- However: **interest-fit meta-analyses** (Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012) found interest congruence correlates more strongly with *performance and persistence* than with satisfaction per se. So "interesting" matters, but it's not the whole satisfaction story.

## Regret research — this cuts in an interesting direction for you

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* far more than actions — chances not taken, paths not pursued. Career regrets are among the most common life regrets.
- A wrinkle for your case: you say leading a team is *something you always wanted*. The regret literature suggests that not pursuing a long-held aspiration is exactly the kind of thing people regret decades later. So "interesting work" isn't the only intrinsic factor in play here — autonomy, growth, and pursuing identity-relevant goals are also intrinsic motivators.

## One bias to watch

- **The focusing illusion (Schkade & Kahneman, 1998)**: "Nothing in life is as important as you think it is while you are thinking about it." When comparing offers, the salient differences (salary, title) get overweighted relative to mundane daily experience — which is mostly determined by what you actually do hour-to-hour.

## How this maps to your situation

The research would frame your choice not as "interesting vs. better package" but as **two competing intrinsic goods**: engaging work (Google) vs. a long-desired growth/leadership goal (OpenAI). The salary difference is, per the literature, nearly noise. The questions research can't answer: how *much* less interesting is the new work (boredom is genuinely corrosive to well-being — see the "boreout" literature), and is the leadership opportunity attainable at Google later, which would make declining it less of a permanent "inaction"?

Want me to go deeper on any of these — e.g., the manager-transition literature specifically?

## Pay vs. intrinsic factors

- **Judge et al. (2010) meta-analysis** ("The relationship between pay and job satisfaction") found a surprisingly weak correlation between salary level and job satisfaction (~r = 0.15) and almost no correlation between pay and *pay satisfaction* across people. People earning very different amounts report similar satisfaction with their jobs.

For someone at Google/OpenAI compensation levels, marginal salary differences likely have negligible well-being effects. (Note: Killingsworth's 2021 follow-up complicated this — life *evaluation* keeps rising with income — but the effect sizes remain small at high incomes.)
- **Hedonic adaptation** research (Brickman, Frederick & Loewenstein) consistently shows people adapt to pay raises within months, while features of daily experience (what you actually do all day) adapt much less because they're re-experienced constantly.

## Interesting/meaningful work specifically

- **Hackman & Oldham's Job Characteristics Model** — one of the most replicated frameworks in organizational psychology — identifies skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback as primary drivers of work motivation and satisfaction. "Interestingness" loads heavily on these.
- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)**: intrinsic motivation (autonomy, competence, relatedness) predicts sustained engagement and well-being far better than extrinsic rewards. There's even evidence extrinsic rewards can *crowd out* intrinsic motivation.
- **Wrzesniewski et al. (2014, PNAS)** studied ~10,000 West Point cadets: those driven by internal motives (interest in the work itself) had better long-term career outcomes than those with instrumental motives (pay, status) — and notably, having instrumental motives *in addition to* internal ones predicted *worse* outcomes.
- **Wrzesniewski's earlier work** on job/career/calling orientations: people who see their work as intrinsically meaningful report higher life satisfaction, controlling for income and occupation.

## Regret research

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: long-term regrets are overwhelmingly about *inactions* — things not tried — rather than actions taken. Relevant in both directions for you, though.
- **Bronnie Ware's hospice observations** (anecdotal, not rigorous) and more systematic surveys consistently find regrets cluster around unfulfilled aspirations rather than insufficient earnings.

## An important complication for your case

The research framing of "interesting vs. money" doesn't map cleanly onto your decision, because **leading a team — something you say you've always wanted — is itself an intrinsic/growth factor**, not an extrinsic one. SDT would classify a long-held aspiration for leadership under competence and autonomy needs. So you're not really trading intrinsic for extrinsic; you're trading one intrinsic good (interesting work) for another (a developmental goal), with money as a minor side payment.

The research can't resolve that trade-off for you, but two findings seem relevant:

1. **Affective forecasting research (Gilbert & Wilson)** shows people systematically mispredict what will make them happy — often overweighting salient novel features (title, leadership) and underweighting daily experience. Worth asking: what will the median Tuesday afternoon feel like in each job?
2. Studies on the **individual-contributor-to-manager transition** suggest satisfaction with management depends heavily on whether you enjoy the *activities* of managing (meetings, unblocking people, politics), not the *idea* of it.

If you want a well-sourced synthesis, 80,000 Hours has a literature review on job satisfaction predictors that covers much of this with citations.

answer:
Great question — there's actually a substantial body of research on this. Here's what I can recall, with the usual caveat that you should verify specific citations:

## Pay matters less than people predict

- **Judge et al. (2010)**, a meta-analysis of ~90 studies, found the correlation between salary level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (~0.15). Pay satisfaction correlates poorly with actual pay — it's more about comparisons and fairness.
- **Hedonic adaptation**: raises produce a satisfaction bump that largely fades within a year or two (work in the tradition of Brickman & Campbell's "hedonic treadmill," and later studies on lottery winners and income changes). A "bit higher" salary is exactly the kind of thing people adapt to fastest.

## Intrinsic interest matters a lot

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)**: well-being at work is driven by autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Intrinsically motivated work strongly predicts engagement and sustained satisfaction; extrinsic rewards don't compensate well and can even undermine intrinsic motivation ("overjustification effect").
- **Interest-fit meta-analyses (e.g., Nye et al., 2012)**: congruence between personal interests and job content predicts both performance and persistence in the role.
- **Wrzesniewski's work on job/career/calling orientations**: people who see their work as intrinsically meaningful report higher life satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement — even within the same occupation.
- **Herzberg's two-factor theory** (old but influential): pay is a "hygiene factor" — its absence causes dissatisfaction, but it doesn't create satisfaction. The work itself, achievement, and growth are the actual "motivators."

## But — the regret literature cuts the other way for you

This is the part worth paying attention to in your specific case:

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* (chances not taken) far more than actions. Short-term, action regrets sting more; over years, "what ifs" dominate.
- **Davidai & Gilovich (2018)** on "ideal-self" regrets: people's most enduring regrets concern failures to pursue their aspirations and become who they wanted to be — more than failures to meet duties and obligations.

You said leading a team is **something you always wanted**. That reframes the question. It's not purely "interesting work vs. money" — leading a team may itself be an intrinsic aspiration. Autonomy and growth (which a leadership role can provide) are exactly the SDT factors that predict satisfaction. Declining it might be the kind of inaction people regret at 50.

## The honest synthesis

The research suggests:

1. Don't move for the salary alone — that effect will evaporate.
2. "Interesting work" is one of the strongest known predictors of sustained job satisfaction. Taking a boring job for extrinsic reasons is a well-documented path to disengagement.
3. **However**, if team leadership is a genuine long-held aspiration (not just status), the regret literature suggests untested aspirations haunt people more than almost anything else.

One practical question the research can't answer but you can: is leadership available to you at Google on a reasonable timeline? If yes, you may not face a real trade-off. If no, you're choosing between two different intrinsic goods — and that's where the inaction-regret findings become most relevant.

answer:
This is a question where there's actually a decent body of research. Here's what I can recall, with the caveat that you should verify citations before relying on them heavily:

## Interest/meaning vs. pay

- **Judge et al. (2010)** ran a meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction and found the correlation is surprisingly weak (~r = .15). Pay *level* barely predicts how satisfied people are with their jobs. Pay satisfaction is more about comparison to peers than absolute amount.
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** famously found day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around ~$75k. **Killingsworth (2021)** challenged this, showing a log-linear relationship continuing higher, and their **adversarial collaboration (Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023)** concluded income keeps mattering for most people — but the effect is logarithmic. Going from one high tech salary to a slightly higher one is a near-zero predicted effect.
- **Hedonic adaptation** research (Lucas, Diener and others) suggests people adapt to pay raises quickly — within months to a year — while intrinsic job features like autonomy and engaging tasks show much less adaptation.

## Intrinsic motivation specifically

- **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan) has decades of evidence that autonomy, competence, and relatedness at work predict well-being, while extrinsically-motivated choices predict worse outcomes even when achieved.
- **Wrzesniewski et al. (1997)** on work orientations: people who see work as a "calling" (intrinsically meaningful) report higher life and job satisfaction than those with "job" (money) or "career" (advancement) orientations.
- **Wrzesniewski & Schwartz (2014)**, the West Point study: cadets with internal motives outperformed and persisted more; adding instrumental motives (money, status) actually *worsened* outcomes.
- **Allan et al. (2019)** meta-analysis on meaningful work: strong correlations with job satisfaction, engagement, and lower withdrawal.

## Regret research

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* more than actions. Career regrets are among the most common life regrets, and they skew toward chances not taken.
- **Steven Levitt (2020)**, the coin-flip study: people facing major life decisions (including job changes) who made the change reported significantly higher happiness 6 months later — suggesting people are biased toward the status quo.

## Two findings that cut the other way (or complicate things)

- **Boyce & Oswald (2012)** found that job promotions, on average, led to *worse* mental health within a few years, not better — mostly through stress and reduced time. Relevant if the appeal of "leading a team" is partly status-driven. Becoming a manager is also a well-documented source of dissatisfaction for engineers who discover they miss building things.
- **Sheldon & Elliot's self-concordance model**: attaining goals only boosts well-being if the goals are genuinely your own (intrinsic), not adopted from external pressure or "shoulds."

## The honest synthesis

The research fairly consistently says: interesting/meaningful work beats marginal pay increases at your income level, and people adapt to salary fast but not to boredom. **However**, your situation isn't a clean "interesting vs. money" tradeoff — you said leading a team is *something you always wanted*. If that's a genuine intrinsic aspiration rather than a status goal, the self-concordance literature suggests pursuing it could matter as much as task interest. The regret literature would also note that "I never tried managing" is the kind of inaction people regret, and management is a somewhat reversible experiment.

The question the research can't answer is whether your desire to lead is intrinsic or internalized status-seeking — that distinction predicts very different outcomes.

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)** research consistently shows intrinsic motivation (interest in the work itself) predicts well-being, persistence, and performance better than extrinsic motivators. There's even evidence that extrinsic rewards can crowd out intrinsic motivation (the "overjustification effect"), though this effect is debated in magnitude.
- **Wrzesniewski et al. (1997)** found that people who see their work as a "calling" (vs. a job or career) report significantly higher life satisfaction — and this orientation was more predictive of satisfaction than income or occupational prestige.

## But — money and your specific situation

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** famously found emotional well-being plateaus around $75k; however, **Killingsworth (2021)** challenged this with experience-sampling data showing well-being continues rising with income (log-linearly). Their 2023 joint "adversarial collaboration" concluded income matters more than Kahneman originally found — but at Google/OpenAI compensation levels, marginal salary differences likely have small well-being effects either way.
- **Hedonic adaptation research** (Brickman, Frederick & Loewenstein) shows people adapt to pay raises within months, but adaptation to the *content* of daily work is much weaker — you re-experience boring or interesting tasks every day.

## The regret literature cuts both ways for you

This is the interesting wrinkle in your case:

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** showed that long-term, people regret **inactions** (things they didn't do) far more than actions. "I never tried leading a team" is a classic inaction regret.
- **Davidai & Gilovich (2018)** found people's most enduring regrets concern their **"ideal self"** — unfulfilled aspirations and growth — rather than failures of duty. You explicitly said leading a team is "something I always wanted." That's exactly the category of thing people report regretting not pursuing.
- On the other hand, **Roese & Summerville (2005)** found career decisions are the #1 domain of life regret, and regret is strongest where opportunity for change existed — meaning a reversible decision (you could likely return to IC research roles) generates less lasting regret than an irreversible one.

## Synthesis from the research

The literature suggests a genuine tension in your case:

1. **Day-to-day happiness** is driven more by interesting work than by pay — the satisfaction research is quite clear there. Less interesting work is a real, daily, non-adapting cost.
2. **Long-term regret** is driven by unpursued aspirations — and the leadership opportunity hits that exactly.
3. The **salary difference** is, per the research, probably the least important factor of the three at your income level.

One more relevant finding: research on management transitions (e.g., work summarized by Gallup and studies on "accidental managers") shows many people who move into management discover they dislike it — but those who *aspired* to it beforehand report better outcomes. The fact that you've long wanted to lead is a favorable predictor.

If you want to dig deeper, the Judge et al. (2010) meta-analysis and the Gilovich regret papers are probably the most directly relevant starting points.

answer:
This is a question where there's actually a decent amount of relevant research. Here's what I can recall, with the caveat that I may get some details slightly wrong — worth verifying citations before relying on them heavily.

## Pay matters less than people expect

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff et al. (2010)**, a meta-analysis of ~90 studies, found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak: around **r = .15**. Pay differences explain very little of the variance in how satisfied people are with their jobs.
- **Hedonic adaptation to raises** is well documented (e.g., Clark, and later work on adaptation to income changes): the satisfaction boost from a salary increase fades substantially within roughly a year.
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found emotional well-being rises with income but plateaus — and you're likely well above that plateau already at either company. (Killingsworth's 2021 follow-up disputes the plateau, but even there the marginal effect at high incomes is small.)

## Intrinsic interest and meaning matter a lot

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)**: decades of evidence that autonomy, competence, and intrinsically motivated work predict well-being more strongly than extrinsic rewards. Deci, Koestner & Ryan's 1999 meta-analysis even found extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation.
- **Hackman & Oldham's Job Characteristics Model**: skill variety, task significance, and autonomy are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and internal motivation.
- **Allan et al. (2019)** meta-analyzed "meaningful work" and found it strongly predicts engagement, commitment, and life satisfaction, and negatively predicts depression and intent to quit.
- Amabile's work on creativity shows intrinsic motivation is a key driver of creative output — relevant if you care about doing your best research.

## Job changes and regret

- **Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy (2005)** documented the **"honeymoon–hangover effect"**: people who switch jobs get a satisfaction spike that typically decays back toward baseline within about a year. So the initial excitement of a new offer is a poor predictor of how you'll feel in year two.
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* more than actions — not taking the leap is regretted more than taking it and being wrong. **Roese & Summerville (2005)** found career is among the top life-regret domains, and regret concentrates where people perceive they had opportunity but didn't act.
- **Gilbert & Wilson's affective forecasting** research: people systematically overestimate how much salary and status changes will affect their happiness, and underestimate adaptation.

## Two complications for your specific case

1. The research contrast isn't cleanly "interesting vs. boring" here. **Leading a team — something you say you've always wanted — is itself an intrinsic motivator.** Sheldon & Elliot's *self-concordance* research shows that pursuing goals aligned with your authentic long-term values predicts well-being. So this isn't purely "intrinsic vs. extrinsic"; it's two different intrinsic goods (interesting work vs. growth toward a desired role) plus a small extrinsic bonus.
2. "Interesting" itself adapts less than pay does, but **boredom at work is a strong predictor of turnover, disengagement, and lower well-being** — so "much less interesting" is a real cost, not a soft one.

## Summary of what the literature suggests

The salary difference will likely matter very little to your happiness within a year. The honeymoon effect means the offer feels more exciting now than it will later. The interestingness gap is the kind of thing that *doesn't* fade with adaptation. But the regret literature cuts the other way: if team leadership is a genuine long-held aspiration (not just status), passing on it is the kind of inaction people tend to regret most.

If you want one practical takeaway grounded in the research: try to disentangle how much of the OpenAI offer's appeal is the salary/prestige (will fade) versus the leadership growth (won't, if it's authentic) — and whether a leadership path is plausibly available at Google on interesting work, which would dominate both options.

- **Boswell et al. (2005, 2009)** documented the "honeymoon–hangover effect": job satisfaction spikes after a voluntary job change, then declines back toward baseline within about a year. So discount how exciting the new offer *feels* right now.
